content:
What is your opnion of the following statement:
```
I don't think that the SEC will do anything,    
because the government and agencies are part of the problem. I think what could trigger could come from the market    
research offices that will find some magic numbers in the hyperscale report. I think the only way hyperscalers survive this
depreciation and Moore's Law issue is to develop their own AI accelerator that could match NVIDIA in price and performance.
```
Perform a deep research to better suport your answer

args.thought:
Planning research approach for user's three claims:

1. **SEC/government inaction claim**: Need to research SEC/PCAOB historical enforcement on depreciation/accounting issues in tech, especially during bubbles (dot-com, 2008). Also need to check for any current SEC scrutiny on AI capex accounting.

2. **Sell-side analyst trigger claim**: Need to research how sell-side analysts have historically exposed accounting irregularities (e.g., Enron, WorldCom, or more subtle cases like depreciation policy changes). Also check current sell-side coverage of hyperscaler depreciation.

3. **Custom silicon survival claim**: Need to research hyperscaler AI accelerator programs (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium/Inferentia, Meta MTIA, Microsoft Maia) and assess their competitiveness vs NVIDIA on price/performance.

This requires web_search for current data on all three fronts.

Breaking down the three claims to research:

1. **SEC inaction claim**: Need evidence of SEC/PCAOB enforcement patterns on depreciation accounting, especially during tech bubbles. Historical precedents matter - did they act on dot-com capex? 2008 financial assets? Also need current signals - any SEC speeches, comment letters, or enforcement actions on AI capex accounting in 2025-2026?

2. **Sell-side analyst trigger claim**: Need to assess whether sell-side has incentive/courage to challenge accounting. Historical cases: Grubman/WorldCom? More relevant: recent analyst reports questioning hyperscaler depreciation. Are analysts currently flagging this, or are they conflicted (investment banking relationships)?

3. **Custom silicon survival claim**: This is the most testable. Need data on: Google TPU generations, Amazon Trainium/Inferentia adoption rates, Meta MTIA deployment, Microsoft Maia. Key metric: what % of AI inference/training workload runs on custom silicon vs NVIDIA? What's the price/performance gap? Can they realistically match NVIDIA?

Starting with web searches on all three fronts.

args.query: SEC PCAOB enforcement depreciation accounting tech companies AI capex 2025 2026

results.title: Audit Enforcement Actions Fall Sharply in 2025 Amid SEC and ...
results.author: sjanifer
highlights:
Enforcement activity against auditors fell sharply in 2025, marking a dramatic shift in the regulatory landscape as both the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) underwent significant leadership changes and adopted new enforcement philosophies, according to a new report from The Brattle Group published on February 26, 2026. The SEC and PCAOB together initiated just 39 enforcement actions against auditors in 2025, a 33% decrease from 58 actions in 2024, according to the annual analysis prepared by Alison Forman and Adam Karageorge. Total monetary sanctions plummeted 66% to $17.9 million, down from $52.2 million the previous year.  ...    The decline was particularly pronounced at the SEC, which brought only two enforcement actions against auditors in  ...    was initiated before former Chair Gary Gensler’s resignation on January 20, 2025. The minimal enforcement activity reflects a year of significant transition.  ...    While the PCAOB imposed the third-highest total penalties in its 21-year enforcement history at $17.7 million, activity dropped precipitously after Chair Erica Williams’ departure. An estimated 84% of PCAOB actions and 98% of penalties were brought before Atkins fired Williams, and she left on July 22. The SEC oversees the audit regulatory board, and the commission announced four new board members on January 30, 2026. George Botic, who became the PCAOB’s acting chair after Williams’ departure, continued in that role until the new chair, Demetrios Logothetis, was sworn in on February 10, 2026. The appointments followed months of uncertainty about the board’s future, including a failed congressional attempt to  ...    Quality control (QC) deficiencies featured more prominently in PCAOB enforcement. Nearly two-thirds of PCAOB actions in 2025 included QC allegations, compared with 39% during 2022–2024. The PCAOB also continued to cite auditing standards  ...    The report highlights a broader change in enforcement philosophy at both agencies. SEC Chairman Atkins has prioritized fraud, manipulation, and cases involving material investor harm rather than technical or lower-level violations. Judge Ryan, in her first speech on February 11, said that she is “far more concerned with the quality and impact of the  ...    At the PCAOB, Acting Chair Botic identified “promoting transparency in the audit process,” instead of “enforcement,” as one of the board’s three primary investor protection pillars. 2026 Outlook The Brattle Group expects SEC audit-related enforcement to increase modestly in 2026 as new leadership settles in, though activity is projected to remain below historical levels. PCAOB enforcement is expected to decline more sharply. Based on pre-Williams patterns, annual enforcement actions could fall to levels typical of the 2018–2021 period, with penalties potentially declining by 85%. Both regulators are expected to heighten scrutiny of non-U.S. auditors, particularly in jurisdictions identified as posing “unique investor risk,” such as China. Get instant access to AI-assisted research, expert-approved answers, and cutting-edge tools like Advisory Maps and State Charts. Try it today and transform the way you work! results.title: SEC Actions Against Accounting and Auditing Firms and PCAOB ...
highlights:
PCAOB Enforcement Activity Fall to Multiyear Lows in 2025  ...    #### Enforcement actions hit nine-year and four-year lows, respectively, during a year of agency leadership transitions. Accounting and auditing enforcement activity fell in 2025 at both the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB), according to two new Cornerstone Research reports, SEC Accounting and Auditing Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review and PCAOB Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review. The SEC brought 68% fewer accounting and auditing actions than in 2024, and the PCAOB finalized 27% fewer actions over the same period. The declines occurred during a year marked by leadership transitions at both agencies. Consistent with SEC trends, PCAOB enforcement activity declined under the new chair. ##### SEC Accounting and Auditing Enforcement Activity in 2025 - The SEC initiated only 10 accounting and auditing enforcement actions in 2025, down from 31 in 2024 and its lowest level in nine years. - Monetary settlements fell sharply to $31 million, down from $907 million in 2024. - Almost all (98%) the $31 million of 2025 settlements was imposed during outgoing Chair Gary Gensler’s final three  ...    “Although enforcement activity often declines during administrative transitions, 2025 activity was significantly lower  ...    Read the full SEC Accounting and Auditing Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review report. ##### PCAOB Enforcement Activity in 2025 - The PCAOB finalized 37 enforcement actions in 2025, down from 51 in 2024 and its lowest number since 2021. - Of those actions, 33 involved the performance of an audit (Auditing Actions), an 18% decrease from the prior year. - Monetary penalties for Auditing Actions totaled $17.6 million, a 50% decrease from 2024. - Firms comprised over two-thirds of total respondents in 2025 Auditing Actions, up from approximately one-half in 2024. - Of the $17.6 million in total monetary penalties imposed in 2025, over 98% were finalized before outgoing Chair Erica Williams stepped down on July 22, 2025. “Consistent with SEC trends, PCAOB enforcement activity declined under the new chair,” said Russell Molter, a principal at Cornerstone Research and report coauthor. “Interestingly, penalties imposed under Chair Williams  ...    Read the full PCAOB Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review report. #### SEC Accounting and Auditing Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review

Read the Report #### PCAOB Enforcement Activity—2025 Year in Review

highlights:

Just because something is used does not mean it is profitable. GAAP refers to economic benefits. Airlines keep old planes around for overflow during Thanksgiving or Christmas, but are  ...    | Source |
| --- | Elder focuses on the hyperscalers, not the pure-play AI companies. He starts with the scale of the depreciation problem: > Internet companies are in denial about getting fat. The advertising silos and data miners of 10 years ago are now infrastructure-heavy and capital-intensive, but their reporting has yet to adjust to the rapid weight gain. This mismatch may become harder to ignore as the value of their spending is written off. Morgan Stanley forecasts that, collectively, Microsoft, Oracle, Meta Platforms and Alphabet could book more than $680bn in depreciation charges over the next four years | Source |
| --- | The hyperscalers have to depreciate both the data centers and the hardware they contain:  ...    | Source |
| --- |

Michael Burry puts the data in a table and notes that: > Understating depreciation by extending useful life of assets artificially boosts earnings -one of the more common  ...    > Though depreciation is a non-cash cost, the money having already been spent, any change to useful life assumptions  ...    Depreciation is applied through time. What matters isn't just the rate, but also whet it starts: > The value of a data centre under construction is held on a company’s balance sheet, but depreciation is only being  ...    income deduction can be very long. Also: capex adds to a company's book value, showing up immediately in the cash flow statement as investments in property, plant and equipment, without a parallel increase in depreciation expense on the  ...    | Source |
| --- |  ...    to rise. Elder is using estimate compiled by Morgan Stanley’s Accounting & Tax desk: > The team splits hyperscaler capex into AI and non AI, smooths out construction-in-progress costs, and allocates the expected lease expenses to property. The team assumes GPUs have a useful life of up to six years and that warehouses  ...    | Source |
| --- | Despite these optimistic assumptions, the graph of depreciation vs. revenue shows depreciation looms large: > Based on Morgan Stanley’s calculations, Alphabet’s depreciation expense could quadruple by its 2028 year-end. Oracle’s 2025 depreciation charge of $4bn might balloon to $56bn by 2029, which would be equivalent to 28 per cent of  ...    It also looms large as a percentage of total expenses by 2029, with ORCL about 42%, META around 35%, MSFT around 32%  ...    > Those sorts of numbers challenge The Street’s assumption that, with the exception of Oracle, hyperscaler operating  ...    | Source |
| --- | The analysts expectations are fed by the companies, so their analyses may not be skeptical enough. If the Morgan  ...    the skepticism: > So far, hyperscaler costs have been doing the opposite of collapsing. Meta said overnight that its total expenses would be up to 44 per cent higher this year at between $162bn and $169bn, and that 2026 capex would be higher by up to 94 per cent at between $115bn and $135bn. Microsoft also raised capex materially, saying it intends to “roughly double our total datacenter footprint over the next two years”. So much for the hyperscalers' accounting problems. The pure-play AI companies have it even worse. The biggest of the  ...    financial crunch point as huge supplier bills start to come due: > More than US$80bn

results.title: The $107 Billion Blind Spot: Using Daloopa’s MCP to Uncover Depreciation Hidden in Big Tech’s Income Statements
highlights:
test-wp.daloopa.com

Home> Resources> Blog  ...    # The $107 Billion Blind Spot: Using Daloopa’s MCP to Uncover Depreciation Hidden in Big Tech’s Income Statements A recent Wall Street Journal article, "Big Tech Accounting Creates a Blind Spot in the AI Boom," highlights a problem that anyone analyzing hyperscaler financials knows intimately: depreciation expense is difficult to find. A recent Wall Street Journal article, “ Big Tech Accounting Creates a Blind Spot in the AI Boom,” highlights a problem that anyone analyzing hyperscaler financials knows intimately: depreciation expense is difficult  ...    footnotes. The problem is where it shows up on the income statement. At Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle, depreciation isn’t disclosed as a standalone line item. It’s allocated across COGS, R&D, and SG&A expenses. For investors trying to understand the true cost of the AI infrastructure buildout, this creates a genuine blind spot. On November 4, 2024, the FASB issued ASU 2024-03, which requires disaggregated disclosure of income statement expenses for public business entities (PBEs). The ASU does not change the expense captions an entity presents on the face of the  ...    disclosures within the footnotes to the financial statements. With these five companies projecting around $695 billion in combined capital expenditure for 2026, an incredible 83% increase from $379 billion in 2025, depreciation is no longer a minor figure. It’s arguably the most important non-cash  ...    OCI). With FY2026 capex guidance of $50 billion (up from $21.2 billion in FY2025), this ratio is poised to rise  ...    Microsoft and Alphabet demonstrated the strongest operating leverage. Despite extensive AI infrastructure expansions, both companies increased operating income quickly enough to reduce depreciation’s share. Microsoft’s 17.1% and  ...    The depreciation story is really a forward-looking capex story. What companies spend on infrastructure today becomes  ...    | Company | FY2025 Capex | 2026E Guidance | YoY Growth |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | | AMZN | $131.8B | ~$200.0B | +52% |
| GOOG | $91.4B | $175–185B | +97% |
| MSFT | $64.6B | ~$140.0B | +117% | | META | $69.7B | $115–135B | +79% |
| ORCL | $21.2B | ~$50.0B | +136% |
| Total | $378.7B | ~$695B | +83% | This $695 billion in 2026 capex will generate depreciation charges for years to come. Assuming a blended 5-year useful  ...    Meta’s CFO explicitly warned investors that “higher depreciation” would be one of the primary drivers of expense growth  ...    | MSFT | 25.4% | 47.4% | +22.0pp | Oracle is already spending more than 100% of its operating cash flow on capex, with free cash flow negative $10 billion in Q2 FY2026 alone. Barclays has warned the company could face liquidity pressure by late 2026. Its bonds, while still  ...    At 94.5%, Amazon generated virtually no free cash flow in FY2025 despite $139.5 billion in operating cash flow. With $200 billion in guided capex for 2026, Amazon will need to either grow operating cash flow proportionally or accept  ...    125% on a guided basis. ## Conclusion The AI infrastructure boom is creating a depreciation time bomb that’s largely invisible on Big Tech’s income statements. As $695 billion in 2026 capex works its way through 5-6 year depreciation schedules, the impact on operating margins will be substantial and it’s being obscured by accounting practices that spread depreciation across cost of revenue, R&D, and SG&A. Morgan Stanley estimates that Microsoft, Oracle, Meta, and Alphabet alone could book more than $680 billion in cumulative depreciation charges over the next four years. Michael Burry has called the practice of extending useful life assumptions to flatten this impact “one of the more common frauds of the modern era.” Whether you agree with that characterization or not, the data is clear: depreciation is the fastest-growing expense line at every major hyperscaler, and it’s hiding in plain sight. highlights:
the world of technology continues to evolve, the conversation around AI hyperscaler depreciation is becoming increasingly pertinent. Michael Burry, best known for his foresight during the 2008 financial crisis, has recently turned his attention to the burgeoning AI sector, raising alarms about the sustainability and longevity of massive data centers and cloud computing infrastructure. The implications of Burry's warnings are significant, not just for investors but for anyone involved in the tech landscape. This article will dissect the nuances of hyperscaler depreciation, explore potential profit opportunities for contrarian investors, and reflect on Burry's cautionary  ...    AI hyperscalers, like Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure, have consistently expanded their capabilities to meet the ever-growing demand for cloud-based services. These entities operate vast data centers filled with servers, which are the backbone of machine learning and artificial intelligence applications. However, maintaining such facilities involves substantial financial commitments, and as Burry points out, the overhead costs may not be sustainable in the long run. The economic model of these giants is predicated on growth, but what happens when that  ...    ### Understanding Depreciation Depreciation, in a conventional sense, refers to the reduction in value of an asset over time. For hyperscalers, this depreciation can manifest in several ways. The rapid pace of technological advancement means that hardware can quickly become obsolete. Newer, more efficient technologies replace older models, leading to significant write-downs in asset value. According to industry experts, the average lifecycle of data center equipment is around three to five years.  ...    Also Read

results.title: The Melting Ice Cube: Michael Burry and the Great AI Depreciation ...
results.author: Shivam Bhotika
highlights:
### From CoreWeave's debt-fueled expansion to Amazon's quiet reversal, the gap between GPU accounting and GPU reality is finally closing with implications for every AI bet on the table. Shivam Bhotika

Jan 22, 2026

11

1

1

Share  ...    In late 2024, Burry, the “Cassandra” of Scion Asset Management, began signalling that the AI boom is built on a foundation of aggressive accounting. His thesis is simple but devastating: the industry is overstating its earnings by billions because it is pretending its hardware lasts twice as long as it actually does. If Burry is right, the “useful life” of an AI chip is the most expensive lie in tech right now. As Moore’s Law stalls and Huang’s Law  ...    Then the hyperscalers changed the rules. Between 2020 and 2024, Amazon (AWS), Google, and Microsoft orchestrated a  ...    draw), and opportunity cost of rack space, older GPUs don’t age gracefully. They become stranded assets. Burry’s estimate, widely circulated but unconfirmed in details, suggests this mismatch will create a cumulative $176 billion understatement of depreciation between 2026 and 2028 across the sector. While the specific figure is difficult to verify, the directional logic is sound. The hyperscalers have collectively committed to over $300 billion in AI infrastructure capex for 2025 alone, with the majority allocated to GPU acquisitions. If even a fraction of this  ...    The first crack appeared in February 2025. In a quiet but seismic reversal, Amazon announced it had completed a new  ...    uncomfortable questions. Either Amazon is being uniquely conservative in its accounting, possible, but out of character  ...    The bull case for six-year depreciation rests on workload diversification and the cascade theory, which we’ll examine in depth later. The bear case, increasingly supported by on-the-ground operational data, is that AI infrastructure  ...    one Tom’s Hardware analysis noted in late 2024, data center GPUs running AI workloads at 60-70% utilization may have  ...    While the hyperscalers have diversified revenue streams to cushion any write-downs, Amazon has retail and advertising,  ...    but its value collapses faster than depreciation schedules predict.  ## When Balance Sheets Meet the Market: The Coming Revaluation The GPU depreciation story stopped being an accounting debate and became a market event in late 2025.  Michael Burry made the opening move: in September his Scion disclosed roughly $1.1B notional in put options against Nvidia and Palantir — about 79.5% of his reported portfolio and by November he shuttered the fund, arguing markets and management estimates were wildly out of sync. He publicly estimated hyperscalers were understating depreciation by roughly $176B across 2026–28.  Shortly after, Jim Chanos turned his attention to CoreWeave. Using public filings and conservative assumptions (even a generous 10-year GPU life), Chanos argued CoreWeave’s numbers didn’t add up: annualized adjusted EBITDA around $3.4B versus roughly $1.2B in interest on an estimated $20B of GPU assets. The market  ...    rotate to hyperscalers or higher-margin software layers. Credit analysts and institutional investors are now asking pointed operational questions (fleet age, generation mix, utilization, rental deterioration) that managements can no longer ignore.  The takeaway is straightforward: markets have shifted the burden of proof. Firms that proactively reset depreciation and provide transparent fleet metrics preserve credibility; those that wait for a market shove will pay a  ...    empirical for me to analyze this situation from the venture lens. results.title: Michael Burry's AI Depreciation Thesis: Accounting Distortions and the Looming Overinvestment Risk in Tech
results.author: AInvest
highlights:
Burry's AI Depreciation Thesis: Accounting Distortions and the Looming Overinvestment Risk in Tech Generated by AI Agent Rhys Northwood Reviewed by David Feng Saturday, Nov 29, 2025 8:36 pm ET3min read

OverviewThe 5 WsOpposite SidesInfobox - Michael Burry argues major hyperscalers inflate earnings by extending AI hardware depreciation cycles, risking overvaluation. - Prolonged depreciation masks $2.9B annual earnings overstatement for Microsoft MSFT--, with cumulative risks reaching $10-12B yearly. - Industry disputes lifespan estimates (2-6 years) as rapid obsolescence challenges 6-year depreciation norms for AI chips. - AI capex boom ($80B Q1 2025) raises bubble parallels, with negative free cash flow signaling valuation risks for  ...    The tech sector's valuation has long been a subject of fascination for investors, but in 2025, a new debate has emerged: the potential accounting distortions created by the rapid depreciation of AI hardware. Michael Burry, the investor known for his 2008 housing crisis short, has turned his attention to the AI infrastructure boom, arguing that major hyperscalers like Microsoft MSFT--, Amazon AMZN--, and Google are artificially inflating earnings by extending the useful life of their computing assets. This thesis, if validated,  ...    Burry's central claim is that hyperscalers are depreciating AI chips-such as Nvidia's GPUs-over 5–6 years, despite their actual economic lifespan being only 2–3 years due to rapid technological obsolescence and physical wear according to analysis. For example, Microsoft's $17 billion in GPU purchases are being depreciated over six  ...    2024 net income according to data. If this pattern persists, the cumulative overstatement could reach $10–12 billion annually, representing 11–14% of reported earnings according to estimates. This discrepancy creates a "$4 trillion accounting puzzle," as companies spread costs over longer periods than the actual useful life of the hardware. By deferring depreciation expenses, hyperscalers [artificially lower  ...    The debate over AI hardware's economic lifespan is far from settled. While Burry and skeptics argue for a 2–3 year  ...    The AI capex boom has already reshaped the tech landscape, with hyperscalers [projecting combined capex  ...    Burry's thesis highlights a critical blind spot in tech valuations: the interplay between accounting practices and technological obsolescence. While hyperscalers argue that their depreciation schedules align with industry standards, the rapid pace of AI innovation suggests these assumptions may be outdated. For investors, the key takeaway is clear: the AI infrastructure boom is not immune to overinvestment risks. As data centers and GPUs become the new "dot-com" assets, the market must grapple with whether these valuations are built on sustainable fundamentals or speculative accounting. The coming months will test the resilience of this sector. If hyperscalers fail to demonstrate meaningful returns on  ...    Address: 330 7th Ave, Suite 902, New York, NY 10001, US

results.title: Microsoft upgrades its self-developed AI chips to reduce dependence on NVIDIA, claiming to outperform Amazon's Trainium and surpass Google's TPU
highlights: claiming to outperform Amazon's Trainium and surpass Google's TPU Microsoft released its second-generation self-developed artificial intelligence (AI) chip, Maia 200, on Monday, the 26th, Eastern Time. This is a core initiative for Microsoft to reduce its dependence on NVIDIA chips and drive its services more efficiently. The chip, manufactured using TSMC's 3-nanometer process, has begun deployment to data  ...    inference system ever deployed by Microsoft," with a performance improvement of 30% per dollar compared to Microsoft's  ...    enterprise-oriented Copilot assistant and AI services, including OpenAI's latest models. According to Scott Guthrie, Maia 200 surpasses similar chips from Google and Amazon in certain performance metrics. The chip's performance at FP4 precision is three times that of Amazon's third-generation Trainium chip, while its FP8 performance exceeds that of Google's seventh-generation TPU. Microsoft has opened a preview version of the Maia 200  ...    This release highlights the fierce competition among tech giants for autonomy in AI computing power. In the context of tight supply and high costs of NVIDIA chips, Microsoft, Amazon, and Google are accelerating their self-developed chip processes, striving to provide cloud customers with lower-cost, more seamlessly integrated alternatives. Microsoft has  ...    After the release of Maia 200, Microsoft's stock price, which had turned positive earlier in the session, expanded its  ...    workloads, providing over 10 petaFLOPS of computing power at 4-bit precision (FP4) and over 5 petaFLOPS of performance  ...    Microsoft's chip initiative started later than those of Amazon and Google, but all three companies share a similar goal: to create cost-effective machines that can seamlessly integrate into data centers, providing cloud customers with cost savings and other efficiency advantages. The high costs and supply shortages of NVIDIA's latest industry-leading chips have spurred competition to find alternative sources of computing power. Google has its Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), which are not sold as chips but provide computing power through its cloud services. Amazon has launched its own AI accelerator chip, Trainium, with the latest version, Trainium 3, released last December. In each case, these self-developed chips can take on some of the computing tasks originally allocated to NVIDIA GPUs, thereby reducing overall hardware costs. According to data disclosed by Scott Guthrie in a blog post, the Maia 200 significantly outperforms its competitors: its FP4 performance is three times that of the third-generation Amazon Trainium chip, and its FP8 performance exceeds that of Google's seventh-generation TPU. Each Maia 200 chip is equipped with high-bandwidth memory that surpasses AWS's third-generation Trainium AI chip or Google's seventh-generation Tensor Processing Unit (TPU). The performance per dollar of this chip is 30% better than the latest generation of hardware currently deployed by Microsoft. Microsoft's Executive Vice President of Cloud and AI, Guthrie, referred to the Maia 200 as "the most powerful self-developed chip among all hyperscale cloud service providers." Notably, this chip uses Ethernet cables for  ...    It has

results.title: Microsoft Maia 200 Rivals Google TPU, xAI Chips
highlights:
xAI Chips

#Microsoft January 27, 2026Author: Łukasz Grochal Microsoft's Maia 200 is the latest in-house AI accelerator from Azure, aimed at powering big model inference without relying too much on Nvidia. Built on TSMC's 3nm process with over 100 billion transistors, it packs 216GB of HBM3E memory at 7 TB/s bandwidth and 272MB on-chip SRAM. Performance-wise, it hits 10,145 teraFLOPS in FP4 (four times Amazon Trainium 3's level), 5,072 teraFLOPS in FP8 (beating Google's TPU v7), and 1,268 teraFLOPS in BF16, all at 880W TDP. What sets it apart is the smart memory setup that keeps model weights local,  ...    racks. It's rolling out first in US data centers for synthetic data gen, reinforcement learning, Microsoft Foundry, and 365 Copilot, with 30% better perf-per-dollar than prior gear. Google's no slouch; their TPU v7 (used for Gemini 3 training) offers solid FP8 at 4,614 teraFLOPS and 192GB HBM3E, though Maia edges it in low-precision inference. Amazon's Trainium 3 lags in FP4/FP8 with lower bandwidth. Elon Musk's xAI is jumping in too, developing custom 3nm chips like X1 for inference while stockpiling Nvidia gear amid shortages. Maia positions Microsoft to compete head-on in the hyperscaler chip race, focusing on efficiency for their ecosystem rather than broad sales. Nodes with four chips per setup are already live, promising lower TCO.  ...    ### Tesla Revives Dojo 3 as AI5 Chip Rivals Nvidia

highlights:
It's running GPT-5.2 in production. And the economics are exactly what you'd expect when  ...    watt out of the silicon than a general-purpose GPU can deliver. The result is a chip that matches NVIDIA Blackwell B200 inference throughput at roughly two-thirds the power consumption.  ...    See how we work

## The 30% cost claim: where the savings actually come from "30% cheaper" is a bold claim. To be fair, it needs unpacking. The savings stack up from four layers, not just faster  ...    Layer 1: Power efficiency. Maia 200 runs at 750W TDP. NVIDIA's Blackwell B200 burns 1,200W or more. That's 37% less  ...    - Maia 200 handles inference. Serving GPT-5.2 responses, running Microsoft 365 Copilot, processing Azure AI endpoints.  ...    ## The custom silicon arms race: Google, Amazon, and the end of NVIDIA's monopoly pricing Microsoft isn't doing this alone. Every major hyperscaler is building custom inference chips now, and the competitive dynamics are fascinating. Google shipped Ironwood (TPU v7) in late 2025 with 10x inference performance over previous generations. Google claims its internal cost of serving AI workloads on TPUs is 30-50% lower than equivalent GPU infrastructure. Ironwood powers Gemini across Search, Workspace, and Cloud. Anthropic committed to deploying over 1 million Ironwood chips in 2026. And here's a number that should make anyone pay attention: Midjourney slashed its monthly compute bill from $2.1 million to under $700K by migrating from NVIDIA A100/H100 clusters to Google TPU v6e. That's $16.8 million in annual savings from one vendor switch. Amazon shifted AWS Bedrock to primarily run on Trainium 3 chips. CEO Andy Jassy has been explicit: "If you want to allow customers to use AI as expansively as they want, you need to bring down the cost of inference." Trainium 3 is Amazon's answer, with 2.52 petaFLOPS at FP8 on TSMC 3nm. AWS customers can already provision Trainium-backed endpoints  ...    Here's how the three hyperscaler chips stack up head-to-head: | Metric | Maia 200 | Google Ironwood (TPU v7) | Amazon Trainium 3 |
